items.q: Why must a recursive function have a base case?
items.correct: Without it, the function calls itself indefinitely, causing a stack overflow.
items.random_wrong: To make the function run faster.
items.why: Students treat the base case as optional ceremony. It's the TERMINATION CONDITION that stops recursion - each call adds a stack frame, and without termination the stack will overflow.

items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.

items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.q: Which technique involves breaking a problem into smaller sub-problems?
items.why: Correct answer: Decomposition. “Abstraction” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the purpose of a trace table?
items.correct: To track variable values during execution
items.why: It's To track variable values during execution. “To compile code” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many distinct shapes are commonly used in basic flowcharts (start/end, process, decision, input/output)?
items.why: The real figure is 4 shapes. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
